title: How a Clean, Tidy Home Can Help You Survive the Atomic Bomb: A Cold War Film from 1954
description:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revis¬≠it¬≠ed some Cold War pro¬≠pa¬≠gan¬≠da that taught upstand¬≠ing Ame
body:
in History |   	April 6th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	


Not too far back, we revis¬≠it¬≠ed some Cold War pro¬≠pa¬≠gan¬≠da that taught upstand¬≠ing Amer¬≠i¬≠can cit¬≠i¬≠zens How to Spot a Com¬≠mu¬≠nist Using Lit¬≠er¬≠ary Crit¬≠i¬≠cism. It‚Äôs a gem, but it has noth¬≠ing on the 1954 film,¬ÝThe House in the Mid¬≠dle. Select¬≠ed¬Ýfor preser¬≠va¬≠tion in the¬ÝNation¬≠al Film Reg¬≠istry by the Library of Con¬≠gress, the short doc¬≠u¬≠men¬≠tary makes the ulti¬≠mate case for clean¬≠li¬≠ness. Bring¬≠ing view¬≠ers to the Neva¬≠da Prov¬≠ing Grounds, the 12-minute film shows what hap¬≠pens when clean, white hous¬≠es are sub¬≠ject¬≠ed to heat waves from an atom¬≠ic blast, ver¬≠sus what hap¬≠pens when a dingy, ill-kept house goes through the same drill. It turns out that neat peo¬≠ple can not only claim moral vic¬≠to¬≠ry (as they always do). They also get to live anoth¬≠er day. Con¬≠sid¬≠er it proof of the sur¬≠vival of the tidi¬≠est.
The film was pro¬≠duced by¬Ýthe Nation¬≠al Clean Up-Paint Up-Fix Up Bureau with sup¬≠port from¬Ýthe¬ÝFed¬≠er¬≠al Civ¬≠il Defense Admin¬≠is¬≠tra¬≠tion. The¬ÝNation¬≠al Paint, Var¬≠nish and Lac¬≠quer Asso¬≠ci¬≠a¬≠tion also appar¬≠ent¬≠ly played a role, sug¬≠gest¬≠ing that cor¬≠po¬≠rate inter¬≠ests were cap¬≠i¬≠tal¬≠iz¬≠ing on wartime fear. Not the first time that‚Äôs hap¬≠pened in Amer¬≠i¬≠ca. Or that last‚Ä¶
If you would like to sign up for Open Culture‚Äôs free email newslet¬≠ter,¬Ýplease find it here. It‚Äôs a great way to see our new posts, all bun¬≠dled in one email, each day.
If you would like to sup¬≠port the mis¬≠sion of Open Cul¬≠ture, con¬≠sid¬≠er mak¬≠ing¬Ýa dona¬≠tion to our site. It‚Äôs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con¬≠tri¬≠bu¬≠tions will help us con¬≠tin¬≠ue pro¬≠vid¬≠ing the best free cul¬≠tur¬≠al and edu¬≠ca¬≠tion¬≠al mate¬≠ri¬≠als to learn¬≠ers every¬≠where. You can con¬≠tribute through Pay¬≠Pal, Patre¬≠on, and Ven¬≠mo (@openculture). Thanks!
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2013.
Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
Watch Ducked and Cov¬≠ered: A Sur¬≠vival Guide to the Post Apoc¬≠a¬≠lypse (A Lit¬≠tle NSFW)
How to Spot a Com¬≠mu¬≠nist¬Ýby Using Lit¬≠er¬≠ary Crit¬≠i¬≠cism: A 1955 Man¬≠u¬≠al from the U.S. Mil¬≠i¬≠tary
Ani¬≠mat¬≠ed Films Made Dur¬≠ing the Cold War Explain Why Amer¬≠i¬≠ca is Excep¬≠tion¬≠al¬≠ly Excep¬≠tion¬≠al
The Red Men¬≠ace: A Strik¬≠ing Gallery of Anti-Com¬≠mu¬≠nist Posters, Ads, Com¬≠ic Books, Mag¬≠a¬≠zines & Films
Watch ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt Be a Suck¬≠er!,‚Äù the 1947 US Gov¬≠ern¬≠ment Anti-Hatred Film That‚Äôs Rel¬≠e¬≠vant All Over Again
How Dis¬≠ney Fought Fas¬≠cism with Pro¬≠pa¬≠gan¬≠da Car¬≠toons Dur¬≠ing World War II & Avert¬≠ed Finan¬≠cial Col¬≠lapse

title: Watch 434 Avant-Garde and Surreal Short Films Online: Salvador Dal√≠, Marcel Duchamp, Luis Bu√±uel and Many More
description: Much has been writ¬≠ten late¬≠ly about the cri¬≠sis in Hol¬≠ly¬≠wood, which has left many appar¬≠ent¬≠ly sure-fire block¬≠busters floun¬≠der¬≠ing, the¬≠ater
body:
Much has been writ¬≠ten late¬≠ly about the cri¬≠sis in Hol¬≠ly¬≠wood, which has left many appar¬≠ent¬≠ly sure-fire block¬≠busters floun¬≠der¬≠ing, the¬≠aters emp¬≠ty, and pro¬≠duc¬≠tion jobs lost. There are many fac¬≠tors in play ‚Äî some of them, as few diag¬≠noses fail to point out, struc¬≠tur¬≠al ‚Äî but can we ignore the pos¬≠si¬≠bil¬≠i¬≠ty of fatigue, per¬≠haps even bore¬≠dom, with film itself? We‚Äôve post¬≠ed in recent years here on Open Cul¬≠ture about the decay of cin¬≠e¬≠ma, the rise of ‚Äúvisu¬≠al muzak‚Äù on Net¬≠flix,¬Ýwhy movies don‚Äôt feel real any¬≠more, and¬Ýwhy movies don‚Äôt even feel like movies any¬≠more. Even if they‚Äôve lim¬≠it¬≠ed their expo¬≠sure to big-bud¬≠get spec¬≠ta¬≠cles, most once-avid cinephiles will have felt all those phe¬≠nom¬≠e¬≠na for them¬≠selves by now, and many will be con¬≠sid¬≠er¬≠ing whether to look for a new art form to enjoy. But some will won¬≠der: maybe there‚Äôs a cure?


	

There could well be, and a brac¬≠ing one. If you seek a re-enchant¬≠ment with film, there could be few bet¬≠ter places to look than in the work of film¬≠mak¬≠ers who have bro¬≠ken that medi¬≠um down to its very com¬≠po¬≠nents and put it togeth¬≠er again in uncon¬≠ven¬≠tion¬≠al ways. Some of the results shocked audi¬≠ences fifty, six¬≠ty, sev¬≠en¬≠ty, even a hun¬≠dred years ago ‚Äî and indeed, some retain that pow¬≠er today.
You can take a jour¬≠ney through the his¬≠to¬≠ry of such exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal, avant-garde, and sur¬≠re¬≠al motion pic¬≠tures with the YouTube playlist at the top of the post, which com¬≠pris¬≠es 434 such videos. The exact num¬≠ber will vary depend¬≠ing upon your region of the world, as well as upon how many of them have come and gone since the playlist‚Äôs cre¬≠ation. What¬≠ev¬≠er the total, not even a fringe-cin¬≠e¬≠ma habitu√© will have seen every¬≠thing on it (at least, not more than once).


	

Long¬≠time Open Cul¬≠ture read¬≠ers may rec¬≠og¬≠nize on the playlist the work of Dadaist Hans Richter and Mar¬≠cel Duchamp, abstrac¬≠tion pio¬≠neer Viking Eggeling, ear¬≠ly fem¬≠i¬≠nist film¬≠mak¬≠er Ger¬≠maine Dulac, and ani¬≠ma¬≠tor (as well as city sym¬≠phon¬≠ist) Wal¬≠ter Ruttmann, not to men¬≠tion Sal¬≠vador Dal√≠ and Luis Bu√±uel.


	

They may or may not already have encoun¬≠tered the cin¬≠e¬≠mat¬≠ic lega¬≠cy of, say, Sh≈´ji Ter¬≠aya¬≠ma, the all-around avant-gardist and provo¬≠ca¬≠teur whose influ¬≠ence is still felt in Japan¬≠ese art today; Stan Brakhage, who forewent even the use of a cam¬≠era and cre¬≠at¬≠ed his own cin¬≠e¬≠ma by manip¬≠u¬≠lat¬≠ing film direct¬≠ly; or Michael Snow, whose Wave¬≠length tells a sto¬≠ry with¬≠out leav¬≠ing a sin¬≠gle room in which very lit¬≠tle hap¬≠pens. But then, after enough of these exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal, avant-garde, and sur¬≠re¬≠al view¬≠ing expe¬≠ri¬≠ences, you‚Äôll remem¬≠ber that there are many ways for a film to tell a sto¬≠ry ‚Äî and much, much more that film can do besides sto¬≠ry¬≠telling, if only we‚Äôd let it.


	

Relat¬≠ed Con¬≠tent:
A Page of Mad¬≠ness: The Lost Avant Garde Mas¬≠ter¬≠piece from Ear¬≠ly Japan¬≠ese Cin¬≠e¬≠ma (1926)
The Evoca¬≠tive¬≠ness of Decom¬≠pos¬≠ing Film: Watch the 1926 Hol¬≠ly¬≠wood Movie The Bells Become the Exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal 2004 Short Film Light Is Call¬≠ing
Watch the Med¬≠i¬≠ta¬≠tive Cinepo¬≠em ‚ÄúH20‚Äù: A Land¬≠mark Avant-Garde Art Film from 1929
Watch 3000 Years of Art, a 1968 Exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal Film That Takes You on a Visu¬≠al Jour¬≠ney Through 3,000 Years of Fine Art
Watch Mesh¬≠es of the After¬≠noon, the Exper¬≠i¬≠men¬≠tal Short Vot¬≠ed the 16th Best Film of All Time
Paul Schrad¬≠er Cre¬≠ates a Dia¬≠gram Map¬≠ping the Pro¬≠gres¬≠sion of Art¬≠house Cin¬≠e¬≠ma: Ozu, Bres¬≠son, Tarkovsky & Oth¬≠er Auteurs
Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: 7 Artists We're Excited to See at EXPO CHICAGO 2026
description: From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary progra
body:
From April 9 to 12, EXPO CHICAGO returns to Navy Pier, hosting hundreds of galleries, site-specific projects, talks, and multi-disciplinary programming both downtown and across the city. This week is one of the most exciting times for the Chicago-area art scene, and we‚Äôre excited to share our annual preview of what we‚Äôre most looking forward to!
Colossal readers also receive 15 percent off single-day admission with the code COLOSSAL26.
Aliza Nisenbaum, ‚ÄúHitomi‚Äù (2022), oil on linen, 66 x 57 inches
1. Aliza Nisenbaum at Anton Kern and Regan Projects
Presented by Anton Kern and Regan Projects, Aliza Nisenbaum‚Äôs vibrant portraiture portrays her subjects in bold chromatics. Nisenbaum‚Äôs smaller-scale works presented at the fair echo one of her larger projects: a celebratory mural titled ‚ÄúReading Circles/ Weaving Dreams/ Seeding Futures‚Äù¬Ýcreated for the Obama Presidential Center.
Tawny Chatmon, ‚ÄúThe Restoration / Made Whole Again‚Äù (2024-2025), embroidery and handstitched threadwork on archival pigment print, 30 x 25 inches. Image courtesy of the artist and Galerie Myrtis
2. Tawny Chatmon at Galerie Myrtis
We‚Äôre always excited for the opportunity to see Tawny Chatmon‚Äôs portraits up close. Galerie Myrtis will be presenting some of the artist‚Äôs newer works, which continue her interest in melding craft techniques with photography. Rather than gold leaf, though, Chatmon embroiders various motifs onto her portraits.
Maya Fuji, ‚Äú1PM: Clean¬∑ÈæçÁ•û„Å®Êô¥„ÇåÂ•≥‚Äù (2026), acrylic and glitter on canvas, 48 x 60 x 1.5 inches
3. Maya Fuji at Charlie James
Referencing a genre of manga popular in the mid-2000s, Maya Fuji‚Äôs Slice of Life peers into the everyday activities of her young characters. Lounging, petting a napping cat, and getting ready for the evening take center stage in these vibrant yet calm paintings.
LaKela Brown, ‚ÄúTwo Overlapping Collard Leaves‚Äù (2026), polyurethane and acrylic, 23 √ó 8.5 √ó 2 inches
4. LaKela Brown at 56 Henry
LaKela Brown has traded in her stark white paint for another monochromatic palette, coating her polyurethane sculptures in black. For her solo presentation with 56 Henry, the artist explores ethnobotany and Black life in America through renditions of collard greens, tobacco, corn, chicken wings, and more.
Gerard Mas, ‚ÄúParty Horn Lady‚Äù (2026), polychrome resin; 53 x 36.5 x 26 centimeters, edition 5 of 7
5. Gerard Mas at Ting Ting Art Space
Longtime Colossal readers will likely recognize this cheeky figure as one of Gerard Mas‚Äô brazen busts. For the past few years, the Barcelona-based artist has been taking a playful, contemporary approach to sculpture, casting spirited women in a variety of witty roles.
Rahma Lhoussig, ‚ÄúPlaytime 2‚Äù (2023), mixed technique on canvas, 47 1/5 √ó 59 3/10 inches
6. Rhama Lhoussig at dmincubator
Merging abstraction and magical realism, Rhama Lhoussig paints vivid domestic scenes in which a recurring figure amuses herself with stuffed bears, flowers, toy blocks, and more. Crayon scribbles and crude line drawings fill the surrounding negative space, firmly placing the works in a dynamic moment of creativity.
Dee Clements, ‚Äúlowers, Vase, Baskets‚Äù (2026), paper, claybord, reed, pine needle, dye, gouache, water-soluble pastel, 31 x 24.5 inches
7. Dee Clements with Mindy Solomon
Chicago‚Äôs own Dee Clements presents a suite of still-life paintings framed in hand-woven frames, alongside mixed-media sculpture and abstract tapestries.

title: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals
description: When we think of somewhere we‚Äôve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people bein
body:
When we think of somewhere we‚Äôve been, what are the first things to come to mind? Perhaps there are memorable smells, a sense of other people being around, or a particular quality of light. But what if we remembered landscapes and experiences through plants? For Hillary Waters Fayle, flower petals, seeds, and foliage combine into a kind of album of various places, which she then uses to create bold cyanotypes.
The artist has long worked with botanicals and other organic materials, notably embroidering foraged leaves and feathers with meticulous geometric designs. With the series Portraits of Place, which she‚Äôs been pursuing for the past six years, Fayle precisely arranges individual petals and leaves into intricate, symmetrical, mandala-like compositions on acrylic.

She starts by collecting and drying botanicals from specific locations, such as Grace Farms Foundation in New Canaan, Connecticut, or Maymont Park in Richmond, Virginia. These are then laid onto watercolor paper that‚Äôs been painted with UV-sensitive iron salts. After being left out in the sun, these result in the bright blue cyanotypes that chronicle the outlines‚Äîperhaps one could even say the spirit‚Äîof the distinctive layouts.
‚ÄúThe way these portraits illustrate a very particular place and time via botany can be a way to define the relationship that the people of that place have with the land‚Äîalmost like a modern-day florilegium,‚Äù Fayle tells Colossal. She continues:

All of these pieces are so different and special to me, but it has been particularly meaningful to make portraits of areas that are going to change drastically in the near future, either from development or rising sea levels, fire, etc. It feels like a way of preserving and honoring the land and all that is present there right now.¬Ý

Flowers and plants symbolize the natural evolution of particular spaces, such as a gardener adding new bulbs or birds depositing seeds. They symbolize the nature of seasons and life cycles, emphasizing a relationship that is simultaneously enduring and ephemeral. See more on the artist‚Äôs Instagram.
Chisman Creek Park, Tidewater, Virginia (detail)

schema.headline: Hillary Waters Fayle Creates 'Portraits of Place' from Seeds, Foliage, and Petals

title: Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds
description:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artist‚Äôs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On
body:
Announcement
								The first US museum exhibition to focus on the artist‚Äôs late work, produced in response to the fascism of the 1930s. On view at the Jewish Museum through July 26, 2026. 

						
								
																
														
								
						
		
	  		    
        
            Paul Klee, "Angel Applicant (Engel-Anw√§rter)" (1939), opaque watercolor, brush and black ink, and graphite on paper mounted on board, 25 3/4 √ó 17 1/2 inches. The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York, The Berggruen Klee Collection, 1984, 1984.315.60. (¬© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
    
		
					
						Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds traces the Swiss-German artist‚Äôs departure from the Bauhaus and his experience throughout the political upheaval of the 1930s prior to his death in 1940. The first US museum exhibition to explore Paul Klee‚Äôs powerful creative output from the final decade of his life, Other Possible Worlds at the Jewish Museum highlights 100 paintings and drawings from across Klee‚Äôs career, including rarely shown works from the 1930s to 1940s. This broader context dramatically frames the artist‚Äôs late practice, during which Klee‚Äôs lifelong individuality and imagination prevail as a form of resistance to Nazi ideology and persecution.Born in 1879 in Switzerland to a music teacher and singer, Klee trained in the violin before shifting to the visual arts during his teenage years. He was involved with a range of burgeoning artistic movements during his early career and went on to establish an esteemed reputation during a decade-long tenure at the Bauhaus. In 1931, seeking to free himself from the demands of lecturing and focus on painting, Klee resigned his position and was offered another at the D√ºsseldorf Academy. However, during Hitler‚Äôs ascent to power, the National Socialists deemed Klee‚Äôs art subversive and degenerate and dismissed him from his position, referring to him as ‚Äúa Galician Jew.‚Äù Forced into exile as an immigrant in his country of birth, Klee abandoned his uplifting chromatic style of painting as he confronted the harsh terrain of fascism and soon, in 1935, the effects of scleroderma, a then-fatal autoimmune disease.Paul Klee, ‚ÄúTropical Blossom (Tropische Bl√ºte)" (1920), oil and pencil on primed paper on cardboard, 10 1/4 √ó 11 5/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern. (¬© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)The exhibition explores this progression, illuminating Klee‚Äôs relentless search for new methods of expressing social critique, non-conformism, mythopoetic thinking, and an evolving approach to developing a new vocabulary for confronting the horrors of political oppression and violence.Curated by Mason Klein, Senior Curator Emeritus, and organized by the Jewish Museum in collaboration with the Zentrum Paul Klee and the Kunstmuseum Bern, Paul Klee: Other Possible Worlds is on view at the Jewish Museum, 5th Ave at 92nd St in New York City, through July 26, 2026.Plan a visit at thejewishmuseum.org. Paul Klee, ‚ÄúUntitled (Last Still Life) (Ohne Titel [Letztes Stilleben])‚Äù (1940), oil on canvas, 39 3/8 √ó 31 11/16 inches. Zentrum Paul Klee, Bern, Livia Klee Donation. (¬© 2026 Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York)
									
											
												Announcement
											
											
												Exhibition Announcement
											
											
												Sponsored
											
											
												New York

title: Art and Springtime in Upstate NY
description:
Daily Newsletter
                Plus, solace in Leah Ke Yi Zheng‚Äôs divine paintings, Ali Cherri accuses Israel of war crimes, a
body:
Daily Newsletter
                Plus, solace in Leah Ke Yi Zheng‚Äôs divine paintings, Ali Cherri accuses Israel of war crimes, and a revealing new survey of POC-led arts orgs.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Spring in Upstate New York might just be my favorite time and place in the world‚Äî¬ÝI love that sense of hope, renewal, and relief as those ceaseless gray days finally surrender to color. Speaking of, there's some pretty great art on view in the region, as our favorite Upstate art writer, Taliesin Thomas, proves in her guide this month.Today, a new survey finds that art museums and organizations led by people of color are disproportionately understaffed, relying heavily on volunteer work to serve their communities. Also in the news, artist Ali Cherri, who won the Silver Lion Award for best film feature at the 2022 Venice Biennale, files a war crimes complaint against Israel for a Beirut strike that killed his parents. If you find yourself in need of a reprieve from earthly matters, Ed Simon and John Yau offer very different approaches to salvation in this issue: Salvador Dal√≠'s "Nuclear Mysticism," on the one hand, and Leah Ke Yi Zheng's contemporary divination on the other. ‚ÄîLisa Yin Zhang, associate editor
Installation view of Sophie Kitching, ‚ÄúBranches‚Äù (2026) (photo courtesy Tyte gallery)10 Exhibitions to See in Upstate New York This AprilLisa Karrer‚Äôs ‚Äúwarm technology,‚Äù Deirdre O‚ÄôConnell‚Äôs loving portraits, Caleb Weintraub‚Äôs fantastical realms, and more. | Taliesin Thomas
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Call for Applications: 2026 Craft Archive FellowshipThe Center for Craft will award up to four $5,000 fellowships to support research on underrepresented craft histories, culminating in an article on Hyperallergic.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            NewsVisitors at the Anacostia Community Museum look at archival protest materials in the 2023 exhibition A Bold and Beautiful Vision: A Century of Black Arts Education in Washington, D.C., 1900-2000 (photo by Matailong Du, courtesy the Anacostia Community Museum and Museum Hue)A new report found that museums and cultural centers founded and led by people of color in the Northeast face severe staffing shortages, with over a third of responding institutions lacking a single full-time employee. Lebanese artist Ali Cherri has formally accused the Israeli military of committing a war crime for a strike that killed his parents in their Beirut apartment in 2024. FeatureSalvador Dal√≠, ‚ÄúThe Sacrament of the Last Supper‚Äù (1955) oil on canvas (photo courtesy National Gallery of Art)Salvador Dal√≠‚Äôs Frustrating Vision of the DivineHaving abandoned the profane for only the sacred, Dal√≠‚Äôs ‚ÄúNuclear Mysticism‚Äù renounced the richness of experience for the aridity of metaphysics. | Ed SimonFrom Our CriticsInstallation view of Leah Ke Yi Zheng,¬ÝChange, I Ching (64 Paintings) (2026) (photo Forrest Frederick for Bob, courtesy the Renaissance Society)Leah Ke Yi Zheng‚Äôs Personal I ChingThe artist is synthesizing the divergent cultural histories of Western oil painting and Eastern ink painting into one. | John Yau
                    
                    
                    
                        
                            Brooklyn Fine Art Print Fair Returns to Powerhouse Arts This WeekOver 50 exhibitors, hands-on programming, and a juried print exhibition make up the fair‚Äôs expanded second edition. April 9‚Äì12, 2026.
                        
                        
                            Learn more
                        
                    
            Antonella Gandini's studio in Monzambano, Italy (photo courtesy Antonella Gandini)A View From the Easel‚ÄúI don‚Äôt foresee certain results, but I welcome the provocation of the material and the form.‚Äù | Lakshmi Rivera AminJozanne Rabyor on Hyperallergic‚Äôs ‚Äú15 Shows to See in New York City This April‚Äù:These round-ups are consistently wonderful. All your writers find old and new artists and galleries and shows to recommend and they comment so enticingly that I inevitably click on the venue and fantasize about adding this, that and the next to my own collection. I have limited funds and even more limited space, so it usually remains a game, but one of my favorites to play.From the ArchiveAli Cherri, ‚ÄúStanding Figure (Pow!)‚Äù (2023), Mask Vili (Kongo), iron, xps, clay, sand, pigments, and pvac glue (image courtesy the artist and Imane Far√®s, Paris)Ali Cherri‚Äôs Muddy AffairThe artist retells the myth of Gilgamesh through a meditation on mud, a primordial material and source of timeless storytelling. | Farah Abdessamad
                  
                      
                        Daily Newsletter
                      
                      
                        Newsletter

title: Israel‚Äôs Venice Biennale Artist Responds to Calls for Exclusion: ‚ÄòI Do Not Support Cultural Boycotts‚Äô
description: Belu-Simion Fainaru, the artist representing Israel at the forthcoming Venice Biennale, responded at length this weekend to continued calls for his
body:
Belu-Simion Fainaru, the artist representing Israel at the forthcoming Venice Biennale, responded at length this weekend to continued calls for his nation‚Äôs ejection from the show.

	‚ÄúAs an artist, I do not support cultural boycotts,‚Äù Fainaru wrote in an extended statement sent to ARTnews. ‚ÄúI believe in dialogue and exchange, especially in challenging times. Art thrives on openness, and any narrowing of that space diminishes it. Embracing diverse perspectives enriches the discourse around art and society, and for this reason my commitment to dialogue has deepened in recent years.‚Äù
	

	
		Related Articles
	


	Fainaru‚Äôs Israeli Pavilion has been protested by groups such as the Art Not Genocide Alliance, which organized an open letter that called Israel a ‚Äúgenocidal state‚Äù and demanded its exclusion from the Biennale.

	‚ÄúAs we reach an appalling anniversary‚Äîtwo and a half years of open genocide against Palestine‚Äîand 77 years after the Nakba, the Israeli state once again seeks the legitimation of the Biennale to masquerade as a creator instead of a destroyer of life and culture,‚Äù the letter reads.

	The letter was signed by dozens of artists in the main exhibition, curated by Koyo Kouoh, as well as two of the curatorial advisers appointed by Kouoh prior to her death last year. Dozens of national representatives, for nations ranging from Belgium to the United Arab Emirates, also signed the ANGA letter, which was followed by a second one focused on Israel in addition to the United States and Russia. The latter nation‚Äôs participation has generated a large outcry, with multiple open letters and a threat by the European Union to defund the Biennale.

	The Biennale has claimed it cannot ask any nation recognized in Italy to exit the show because it ‚Äúrejects any form of exclusion or censorship of culture and art.‚Äù In a statement from March, the Biennale said this was because the show is ‚Äúa place of dialogue, openness, and artistic freedom.‚Äù (Palestine has never had a national pavilion at the Biennale because it is not recognized as a country within Italy. This year‚Äôs collateral exhibitions‚ÄîBiennale-sanctioned shows held outside the official exhibition‚Äîinclude one about Gaza that is organized by the Palestine Museum US.)
	

	Fainaru, who told ARTnews that he submitted his statement to Biennale president Pietrangelo Buttafuoco, seemed at times to echo the Biennale‚Äôs words in his own statement.

	‚ÄúI perceive art as a universal language through which individuality takes shape and human connection becomes possible,‚Äù Fainaru wrote. ‚ÄúThroughout history, art has reflected the diversity of our world, yet its true value lies in its openness: art belongs to everyone, transcending backgrounds, beliefs, and borders.‚Äù

	Later in his statement, Fainaru wrote, ‚ÄúI value the freedom of expression and welcome a plurality of voices and perspectives. I am here to share my work and to remain open to dialogue. The Israeli Pavilion welcomes all who come in a genuine spirit of exchange. I hope visitors will bring their own experiences and perspectives, allowing the work to become a space for thoughtful engagement.‚Äù

	Moreover, he described his exhibition, an installation called Rose of Nothingness that will feature a reflective pool filled with darkened liquid, as a reminder that ‚Äúlife, like art, is not created by accumulation or excess, but by listening to what is absent, to what is still becoming.‚Äù

	The last artist to represent Israel at the Biennale was Ruth Patir, who closed her 2024 pavilion to the public on opening day. She said it would only reopen upon the establishment of a ceasefire in Gaza and a deal to free the hostages taken by Hamas, neither of which happened during the Biennale‚Äôs run.

schema.headline: Israel‚Äôs Venice Biennale Artist Responds to Calls for Exclusion: ‚ÄòI Do Not Support Cultural Boycotts‚Äô
